problems.title: Erdos Problem #1
problems.statement_preview: If $A\subseteq \{1,\ldots,N\}$ with $|A|=n$ has all subset sums
problems.established_preview: - Major progress known, original stronger form still open.
problems.references_preview: - Classical lower bounds are much weaker than $N\gg 2^n$:

problems.title: Erdos Problem #3
problems.statement_preview: Investigate the Erdős-Turán type principle linking additive largeness and arithmetic progressions:
problems.established_preview: - Partially resolved in literature at low progression lengths.
problems.references_preview: - For 3-term progressions, quantitative Roth-type results are very strong.

problems.title: Erdos Problem #5
problems.statement_preview: Let
problems.established_preview: - Substantial partial structure known; full characterization remains open.
problems.references_preview: - Known: both $0$ and $\infty$ occur as limit points.

problems.title: Erdos Problem #9
problems.statement_preview: Let $A$ be the set of all odd integers not of the form $p+2^{k}+2^l$ (where $k,l\geq 0$ and $p$ is prime). Is the upper density of $A$ positive?
problems.established_preview: Strong partial lower bounds, density positivity still open.
problems.references_preview: In \cite{Er77c} Erdos credits Schinzel with proving that there are infinitely many odd integers not of this form, but gives no reference. Crocker \cite{Cr71} has proved there are $\gg\log\log N$ such integers in $\{1,\ldots,N\}$. Pan \cite{Pa11} improved this to $\gg_\epsilon N^{1-\epsilon}$ for any $\epsilon>0$. Erdos believed this cannot be proved by covering systems, i.e. i…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #10
problems.statement_preview: Is there some $k$ such that every integer is the sum of a prime and at most $k$ powers of 2?
problems.established_preview: Very strong finite evidence for small `k` (not a proof).
problems.references_preview: Erdos described this as 'probably unattackable'. In \cite{ErGr80} Erdos and Graham suggest that no such $k$ exists. Gallagher \cite{Ga75} has shown that for any $\epsilon>0$ there exists $k(\epsilon)$ such that the set of integers which are the sum of a prime and at most $k(\epsilon)$ many powers of 2 has lower density at least $1-\epsilon$. Granville and Soundararajan \cite{G…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #12
problems.statement_preview: Let $A$ be an infinite set such that there are no distinct $a,b,c\in A$ such that $a\mid (b+c)$ and $b,c>a$. Is there such an $A$ with $ \liminf \frac{\lvert A\cap\{1,\ldots,N\}\rvert}{N^{1/2}}>0? $ Does there exist some absolute constant $c>0$ such that there are always infinitely many $N$ with $ \lvert A\cap\{1,\ldots,N\}\rvert<N^{1-c}? $ Is it true that $ \sum_{n\in A}\frac…
problems.established_preview: Partially resolved with strong constructions and upper constraints; central growth/reciprocal questions remain open.
problems.references_preview: Asked by Erdos and S\'{a}rk"{o}zy \cite{ErSa70}, who proved that $A$ must have density $0$. They also prove that this is essentially best possible, in that given any function $f(x)\to \infty$ as $x\to \infty$ there exists a set $A$ with this property and infinitely many $N$ such that $ \lvert A\cap\{1,\ldots,N\}\rvert>\frac{N}{f(N)}. $ (Their example is given by all integers i…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #14
problems.statement_preview: Let $A\subseteq \mathbb{N}$. Let $B\subseteq \mathbb{N}$ be the set of integers which are representable in exactly one way as the sum of two elements from $A$. Is it true that for all $\epsilon>0$ and large $N$ $ \lvert \{1,\ldots,N\}\backslash B\rvert \gg_\epsilon N^{1/2-\epsilon}? $ Is it possible that $ \lvert \{1,\ldots,N\}\backslash B\rvert =o(N^{1/2})? $
problems.established_preview: Open in this batch; finite evidence aligns with known `N^{1/2}`-type scale.
problems.references_preview: Apparently originally considered by Erdos and Nathanson, although later Erdos attributes this to Erdos, S\'{a}rk"{o}zy, and Szemer\'{e}di (but gives no reference), and claims a construction of an $A$ such that for all $\epsilon>0$ and all large $N$ $ \lvert \{1,\ldots,N\}\backslash B\rvert \ll_\epsilon N^{1/2+\epsilon}, $ and yet there for all $\epsilon>0$ there exist infinite…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #15
problems.statement_preview: Is it true that $ \sum_{n=1}^\infty(-1)^n\frac{n}{p_n} $ converges, where $p_n$ is the sequence of primes?
problems.established_preview: Numerical behavior is compatible with convergence; unconditional proof remains open.
problems.references_preview: Erdos suggested that a computer could be used to explore this, and did not see any other method to attack this. Tao \cite{Ta23} has proved that this series does converge assuming a strong form of the Hardy-Littlewood prime tuples conjecture. In \cite{Er98} Erdos further conjectures that $ \sum_{n=1}^\infty (-1)^n \frac{1}{n(p_{n+1}-p_n)} $ converges and $ \sum_{n=1}^\infty (-1…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #17
problems.statement_preview: Are there infinitely many primes $p$ such that every even number $n\leq p-3$ can be written as a difference of primes $n=q_1-q_2$ where $q_1,q_2\leq p$?
problems.established_preview: Strong finite positive evidence, infinitude still unproved.
problems.references_preview: The first prime without this property is $97$. The sequence of such primes is A038133 in the OEIS. These are called cluster primes. Blecksmith, Erdos, and Selfridge \cite{BES99} proved that the number of such primes is $ \ll_A \frac{x}{(\log x)^A} $ for every $A>0$, and Elsholtz \cite{El03} improved this to $ \ll x\exp(-c(\log\log x)^2) $ for every $c<1/8$. This is discussed i…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #18
problems.statement_preview: We call $m$ practical if every integer $n<m$ is the sum of distinct divisors of $m$. If $m$ is practical then let $h(m)$ be such that $h(m)$ many divisors always suffice. Are there infinitely many practical $m$ such that $h(m)<(\log\log m)^{O(1)}$? Is it true that $h(n!)<n^{o(1)}$? Or perhaps even $h(n!)<(\log n)^{O(1)}$?
problems.established_preview: No full resolution; supports plausibility of slow growth but not a theorem.
problems.references_preview: It is easy to see that almost all numbers are not practical. Erdos originally showed that $h(n!)<n$. Vose \cite{Vo85} proved the existence of infinitely many practical $m$ such that $h(m)\ll (\log m)^{1/2}$. The sequence of practical numbers is A005153 in the OEIS. A reward of USD 250 is offered in \cite{Er81h}, apparently (although this is not entirely clear) for a proof or d…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #20
problems.statement_preview: Let $f(n,k)$ be minimal such that every family $\mathcal{F}$ of $n$-uniform sets with $\lvert \mathcal{F}\rvert \geq f(n,k)$ contains a $k$-sunflower. Is it true that $ f(n,k) < c_k^n $ for some constant $c_k>0$?
problems.references_preview: Erdos and Rado \cite{ErRa60} originally proved $f(n,k)\leq (k-1)^nn!$. Kostochka \cite{Ko97} improved this slightly (in particular establishing an upper bound of $o(n!)$, for which Erdos awarded him the consolation prize of \$100), but the bound stood at $n^{(1+o(1))n}$ for a long time until Alweiss, Lovett, Wu, and Zhang \cite{ALWZ20} proved $ f(n,k) < (Ck\log n\log\log n)^n …

problems.title: Erdos Problem #25
problems.statement_preview: Let $n_1<n_2<\cdots$ be an arbitrary sequence of integers, each with an associated residue class $a_i\pmod{n_i}$. Let $A$ be the set of integers $n$ such that for every $i$ either $n<n_i$ or $n ot\equiv a_i\pmod{n_i}$. Must the logarithmic density of $A$ exist?
problems.references_preview: This is a special case of [486].

problems.title: Erdos Problem #28
problems.statement_preview: If $A\subseteq \mathbb{N}$ is such that $A+A$ contains all but finitely many integers then $\limsup 1_A\ast 1_A(n)=\infty$.
problems.references_preview: Conjectured by Erdos and Tur\'{a}n. They also suggest the stronger conjecture that $\limsup 1_A\ast 1_A(n)/\log n>0$. Another stronger conjecture would be that the hypothesis $\lvert A\cap [1,N]\rvert \gg N^{1/2}$ for all large $N$ suffices. Erdos and S\'{a}rk"{o}zy conjectured the stronger version that if $A=\{a_1<a_2<\cdots\}$ and $B=\{b_1<b_2<\cdots\}$ with $a_n/b_n\to 1$ a…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #30
problems.statement_preview: Let $h(N)$ be the maximum size of a Sidon set in $\{1,\ldots,N\}$. Is it true that, for every $\epsilon>0$, $ h(N) = N^{1/2}+O_\epsilon(N^\epsilon)? $
problems.references_preview: A problem of Erdos and Tur\'{a}n. It may even be true that $h(N)=N^{1/2}+O(1)$, but Erdos remarks this is perhaps too optimistic. Erdos and Tur\'{a}n \cite{ErTu41} proved an upper bound of $N^{1/2}+O(N^{1/4})$, with an alternative proof by Lindstr"{o}m \cite{Li69}. Both proofs in fact give $ h(N) \leq N^{1/2}+N^{1/4}+1. $ Balogh, F"{u}redi, and Roy \cite{BFR21} improved the bo…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #32
problems.statement_preview: Is there a set $A\subset\mathbb{N}$ such that $ \lvert A\cap\{1,\ldots,N\}\rvert = o((\log N)^2) $ and such that every large integer can be written as $p+a$ for some prime $p$ and $a\in A$? Can the bound $O(\log N)$ be achieved? Must such an $A$ satisfy $ \liminf \frac{\lvert A\cap\{1,\ldots,N\}\rvert}{\log N}> 1? $
problems.references_preview: Such a set is called an additive complement to the primes. Erdos \cite{Er54} proved that such a set $A$ exists with $\lvert A\cap\{1,\ldots,N\}\rvert\ll (\log N)^2$ (improving a previous result of Lorentz \cite{Lo54} who achieved $\ll (\log N)^3$). Wolke \cite{Wo96} has shown that such a bound is almost true, in that we can achieve $\ll (\log N)^{1+o(1)}$ if we only ask for al…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #33
problems.statement_preview: Let $A\subset\mathbb{N}$ be such that every large integer can be written as $n^2+a$ for some $a\in A$ and $n\geq 0$. What is the smallest possible value of $ \limsup \frac{\lvert A\cap\{1,\ldots,N\}\rvert}{N^{1/2}}? $ Is $ \liminf \frac{\lvert A\cap\{1,\ldots,N\}\rvert}{N^{1/2}}>1? $
problems.references_preview: Such a set $A$ is called an additive complement of the set of squares. Erdos observed that there exist $A$ for which the $\limsup$ is finite and $>1$. Moser \cite{Mo65} proved that, for any such $A$, $ \liminf \frac{\lvert A\cap\{1,\ldots,N\}\rvert}{N^{1/2}}>1.06. $ The best-known lower bound is $ \liminf \frac{\lvert A\cap\{1,\ldots,N\}\rvert}{N^{1/2}}\geq\frac{4}{\pi}\approx…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #36
problems.statement_preview: Find the optimal constant $c>0$ such that the following holds. For all sufficiently large $N$, if $A\sqcup B=\{1,\ldots,2N\}$ is a partition into two equal parts, so that $\lvert A\rvert=\lvert B\rvert=N$, then there is some $x$ such that the number of solutions to $a-b=x$ with $a\in A$ and $b\in B$ is at least $cN$.
problems.references_preview: The minimum overlap problem. The example (with $N$ even) $A=\{N/2+1,\ldots,3N/2\}$ shows that $c\leq 1/2$ (indeed, Erdos initially conjectured that $c=1/2$). The lower bound of $c\geq 1/4$ is trivial, and Scherk improved this to $1-1/\sqrt{2}=0.29\cdots$. The current records are $ 0.379005 < c < 0.380924, $ the lower bound due to White \cite{Wh22} and the upper bound due to Al…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #38
problems.statement_preview: Ask for a set B that is not an additive basis but still guarantees a uniform positive
problems.established_preview: Background gives density increment when B is an additive basis of finite order k.
problems.references_preview: Erdos \cite{Er36c} proved that if $B$ is an additive basis of order $k$ then, for any set $A$ of Schnirelmann density $\alpha$, for every $N$ there exists some integer $b\in B$ such that $ \lvert (A\cup (A+b))\cap \{1,\ldots,N\}\rvert\geq \left(\alpha+\frac{\alpha(1-\alpha)}{2k}\right)N. $ It seems an interesting question (not one that Erdos appears to have asked directly, alt…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #39
problems.statement_preview: Is there an infinite Sidon set $A\subset \mathbb{N}$ such that $ \lvert A\cap \{1\ldots,N\}\rvert \gg_\epsilon N^{1/2-\epsilon} $ for all $\epsilon>0$?
problems.references_preview: The trivial greedy construction achieves $\gg N^{1/3}$. The first improvement on this was achieved by Ajtai, Koml\'{o}s, and Szemer\'{e}di \cite{AKS81b}, who found an infinite Sidon set with growth rate $\gg (N\log N)^{1/3}$. The current best bound of $\gg N^{\sqrt{2}-1+o(1)}$ is due to Ruzsa \cite{Ru98}. Erdos \cite{Er73} had offered \$25 for any construction which achieves $…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #40
problems.statement_preview: For what functions $g(N)\to \infty$ is it true that $ \lvert A\cap \{1,\ldots,N\}\rvert \gg \frac{N^{1/2}}{g(N)} $ implies $\limsup 1_A\ast 1_A(n)=\infty$?
problems.references_preview: This is a stronger form of the Erdos-Tur\'{a}n conjecture [28] (since establishing this for any function $g(N)\to \infty$ would imply a positive solution to [28]).

problems.title: Erdos Problem #41
problems.statement_preview: Let $A\subset\mathbb{N}$ be an infinite set such that the triple sums $a+b+c$ are all distinct for $a,b,c\in A$ (aside from the trivial coincidences). Is it true that $ \liminf \frac{\lvert A\cap \{1,\ldots,N\}\rvert}{N^{1/3}}=0? $
problems.references_preview: Erdos proved that if the pairwise sums $a+b$ are all distinct aside from the trivial coincidences then $ \liminf \frac{\lvert A\cap \{1,\ldots,N\}\rvert}{N^{1/2}}=0. $ This is discussed in problem C11 of Guy's collection \cite{Gu04}, in which Guy says Erdos offered \$500 for the general problem of whether, for all $h\geq 2$, $ \liminf \frac{\lvert A\cap \{1,\ldots,N\}\rvert}{N…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #42
problems.statement_preview: Let $M\geq 1$ and $N$ be sufficiently large in terms of $M$. Is it true that for every Sidon set $A\subset \{1,\ldots,N\}$ there is another Sidon set $B\subset \{1,\ldots,N\}$ of size $M$ such that $(A-A)\cap(B-B)=\{0\}$?
problems.established_preview: Not proved/disproved. For $M=3$, finite behavior is now rigorously

problems.title: Erdos Problem #43
problems.statement_preview: If $A,B\subset \{1,\ldots,N\}$ are two Sidon sets such that $(A-A)\cap(B-B)=\{0\}$ then is it true that $ \binom{\lvert A\rvert}{2}+\binom{\lvert B\rvert}{2}\leq\binom{f(N)}{2}+O(1), $ where $f(N)$ is the maximum possible size of a Sidon set in $\{1,\ldots,N\}$? If $\lvert A\rvert=\lvert B\rvert$ then can this bound be improved to $ \binom{\lvert A\rvert}{2}+\binom{\lvert B\rv…
problems.references_preview: Since it is known that $f(N)\sim \sqrt{N}$ (see [30]) the latter question is equivalent to asking whether, if $\lvert A\rvert=\lvert B\rvert$, $ \lvert A\rvert \leq \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}-c+o(1)\right)\sqrt{N} $ for some constant $c>0$. In the comments Tao has given a proof of this upper bound without the $-c$. In the comments Barreto has given a negative answer to the secon…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #44
problems.statement_preview: Let $N\geq 1$ and $A\subset \{1,\ldots,N\}$ be a Sidon set. Is it true that, for any $\epsilon>0$, there exist $M$ and $B\subset \{N+1,\ldots,M\}$ (which may depend on $N,A,\epsilon$) such that $A\cup B\subset \{1,\ldots,M\}$ is a Sidon set of size at least $(1-\epsilon)M^{1/2}$?
problems.references_preview: See also [329] and [707] (indeed a positive solution to [707] implies a positive solution to this problem, which in turn implies a positive solution to [329]). This is discussed in problem C9 of Guy's collection \cite{Gu04}. References [Gu04] Guy, Richard K., Unsolved problems in number theory. (2004), xviii+437.

problems.title: Erdos Problem #50
problems.statement_preview: Schoenberg proved that for every $c\in [0,1]$ the density of $ \{ n\in \mathbb{N} : \phi(n)<cn\} $ exists. Let this density be denoted by $f(c)$. Is it true that there are no $x$ such that $f'(x)$ exists and is positive?
problems.established_preview: Partially resolved structurally (singularity known), full derivative claim still open.
problems.references_preview: Erdos \cite{Er95} could prove the distribution function is purely singular. References [Er95] Erdos, Paul, Some of my favourite problems in number theory, combinatorics, and geometry. Resenhas (1995), 165-186.

problems.title: Erdos Problem #51
problems.statement_preview: Is there an infinite set $A\subset \mathbb{N}$ such that for every $a\in A$ there is an integer $n$ such that $\phi(n)=a$, and yet if $n_a$ is the smallest such integer then $n_a/a\to \infty$ as $a\to\infty$?
problems.references_preview: Carmichael has asked whether there is an integer $t$ for which $\phi(n)=t$ has exactly one solution. Erdos has proved that if such a $t$ exists then there must be infinitely many such $t$. See also [694]. This is discussed in problems B36 and B39 of Guy's collection \cite{Gu04}. References [Gu04] Guy, Richard K., Unsolved problems in number theory. (2004), xviii+437.

problems.title: Erdos Problem #52
problems.statement_preview: Let $A$ be a finite set of integers. Is it true that for every $\epsilon>0$ $ \max( \lvert A+A\rvert,\lvert AA\rvert)\gg_\epsilon \lvert A\rvert^{2-\epsilon}? $
problems.references_preview: The sum-product problem. Erdos and Szemer\'{e}di \cite{ErSz83} proved a lower bound of $\lvert A\rvert^{1+c}$ for some constant $c>0$, and an upper bound of $ \lvert A\rvert^2 \exp\left(-c\frac{\log\lvert A\rvert}{\log\log \lvert A\rvert}\right) $ for some constant $c>0$. The lower bound has been improved a number of times. The current record is $ \max( \lvert A+A\rvert,\lvert…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #60
problems.statement_preview: Does every graph on $n$ vertices with $>\mathrm{ex}(n;C_4)$ edges contain $\gg n^{1/2}$ many copies of $C_4$?
problems.references_preview: Conjectured by Erdos and Simonovits, who could not even prove that at least $2$ copies of $C_4$ are guaranteed. The behaviour of $\mathrm{ex}(n;C_4)$ is the subject of [765]. He, Ma, and Yang \cite{HeMaYa21} have proved this conjecture when $n=q^2+q+1$ for some even integer $q$. References [HeMaYa21] He, J. and Ma, J. and Yang, T., Some extremal results on 4-cycles. Journal of…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #61
problems.statement_preview: For any graph $H$ is there some $c=c(H)>0$ such that every graph $G$ on $n$ vertices that does not contain $H$ as an induced subgraph contains either a complete graph or independent set on $\geq n^c$ vertices?
problems.references_preview: Conjectured by Erdos and Hajnal \cite{ErHa89}, who proved that a complete graph or independent set must exist on $ \geq \exp(c_H\sqrt{\log n}) $ many vertices, where $c_H>0$ is some constant. This was improved by Buci\'{c}, Nguyen, Scott, and Seymour \cite{BNSS23} to $ \geq \exp(c_H\sqrt{\log n\log\log n}). $ See also the entry in the graphs problem collection. References [BNS…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #62
problems.statement_preview: If $G_1,G_2$ are two graphs with chromatic number $\aleph_1$ then must there exist a graph $G$ whose chromatic number is $4$ (or even $\aleph_0$) which is a subgraph of both $G_1$ and $G_2$?
problems.established_preview: Open in this batch; no proof/counterexample.
problems.references_preview: Erdos also asked \cite{Er87} about finding a common subgraph $H$ (with chromatic number either $4$ or $\aleph_0$) in any finite collection of graphs with chromatic number $\aleph_1$. Every graph with chromatic number $\aleph_1$ contains all sufficiently large odd cycles (which have chromatic number $3$), see [594]. This was proved by Erdos, Hajnal, and Shelah \cite{EHS74}. Erd…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #65
problems.statement_preview: Let $G$ be a graph with $n$ vertices and $kn$ edges, and $a_1<a_2<\cdots $ be the lengths of cycles in $G$. Is it true that $ \sum\frac{1}{a_i}\gg \log k? $ Is the sum $\sum\frac{1}{a_i}$ minimised when $G$ is a complete bipartite graph?
problems.references_preview: A problem of Erdos and Hajnal. Gy\'{a}rf\'{a}s, Koml\'{o}s, and Szemer\'{e}di \cite{GKS84} have proved that this sum is $\gg \log k$, so that only the second question remains. Liu and Montgomery \cite{LiMo20} have proved the asymptotically sharp lower bound of $\geq (\tfrac{1}{2}-o(1))\log k$. See also the entry in the graphs problem collection. See also [57]. References [GKS8…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #66
problems.statement_preview: Is there $A\subseteq \mathbb{N}$ such that $ \lim_{n\to \infty}\frac{1_A\ast 1_A(n)}{\log n} $ exists and is $ eq 0$?
problems.references_preview: A suitably constructed random set has this property if we are allowed to ignore an exceptional set of density zero. The challenge is obtaining this with no exceptional set. Erdos believed the answer should be no. Erdos and S\'{a}rk"{o}zy proved that $ \frac{\lvert 1_A\ast 1_A(n)-\log n\rvert}{\sqrt{\log n}}\to 0 $ is impossible. Erdos suggests it may even be true that the $\li…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #68
problems.statement_preview: Is $ \sum_{n\geq 2}\frac{1}{n!-1} $ irrational?
problems.established_preview: Open; no full irrationality proof or rationality counterexample found.
problems.references_preview: The decimal expansion is A331373 in the OEIS. Weisenberg has observed that this sum can also be written as $ \sum_{k\geq 1}\sum_{n\geq 2}\frac{1}{(n!)^k}. $ Erdos \cite{Er88c} notes that $\sum \frac{1}{n!+t}$ should be transcendental for every integer $t$. References [Er88c] Erd"{o}s, P., On the irrationality of certain series: problems and results. New advances in transcenden…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #70
problems.statement_preview: Let $\mathfrak{c}$ be the ordinal of the real numbers, $\beta$ be any countable ordinal, and $2\leq n<\omega$. Is it true that $\mathfrak{c}\to (\beta, n)_2^3$?
problems.established_preview: No new theorem or counterexample in this batch.
problems.references_preview: Erdos and Rado proved that $\mathfrak{c}\to (\omega+n,4)_2^3$ for any $2\leq n<\omega$.

problems.statement_preview: Given `f(n)->infinity`, is there an infinite-chromatic graph where every
problems.references_preview: Conjectured by Erdos, Hajnal, and Szemer\'{e}di \cite{EHS82}. R"{o}dl \cite{Ro82} has proved this for hypergraphs, and also proved there is such a graph (with chromatic number $\aleph_0$) if $f(n)=\epsilon n$ for any fixed constant $\epsilon>0$. It is open even for $f(n)=\sqrt{n}$. Erdos offered \$500 for a proof but only \$250 for a counterexample. This fails (even with $f(n)…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #75
problems.statement_preview: Is there a graph of chromatic number $\aleph_1$ such that for all $\epsilon>0$ if $n$ is sufficiently large and $H$ is a subgraph on $n$ vertices then $H$ contains an independent set of size $>n^{1-\epsilon}$?
problems.established_preview: No constructive example or obstruction obtained in this batch.
problems.references_preview: Conjectured by Erdos, Hajnal, and Szemer\'{e}di \cite{EHS82}. In \cite{Er95d} Erdos suggests this may even be true with an independent set of size $\gg n$. See also [750]. References [EHS82] Erdos, P. and Hajnal, A. and Szemer\'{e}di, E., On almost bipartite large chromatic graphs. Theory and practice of combinatorics (1982), 117-123. [Er95d] Erdos, Paul, On some problems in c…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #77
problems.statement_preview: If $R(k)$ is the Ramsey number for $K_k$, the minimal $n$ such that every $2$-colouring of the edges of $K_n$ contains a monochromatic copy of $K_k$, then find the value of $ \lim_{k\to \infty}R(k)^{1/k}. $
problems.references_preview: Erdos offered \$100 for just a proof of the existence of this constant, without determining its value. He also offered \$1000 for a proof that the limit does not exist, but says 'this is really a joke as [it] certainly exists'. (In \cite{Er88} he raises this prize to \$10000). Erdos proved $ \sqrt{2}\leq \liminf_{k\to \infty}R(k)^{1/k}\leq \limsup_{k\to \infty}R(k)^{1/k}\leq 4…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #78
problems.statement_preview: Give a constructive proof that $R(k)>C^k$ for some constant $C>1$.
problems.references_preview: Erdos gave a simple probabilistic proof that $R(k) \gg k2^{k/2}$. Equivalently, this question asks for an explicit construction of a graph on $n$ vertices which does not contain any clique or independent set of size $\geq c\log n$ for some constant $c>0$. In \cite{Er69b} Erdos asks for even a construction whose largest clique or independent set has size $o(n^{1/2})$, which is …

problems.title: Erdos Problem #80
problems.statement_preview: Let $c>0$ and let $f_c(n)$ be the maximal $m$ such that every graph $G$ with $n$ vertices and at least $cn^2$ edges, where each edge is contained in at least one triangle, must contain a book of size $m$, that is, an edge shared by at least $m$ different triangles. Estimate $f_c(n)$. In particular, is it true that $f_c(n)>n^{\epsilon}$ for some $\epsilon>0$? Or $f_c(n)\gg \log…
problems.references_preview: A problem of Erdos and Rothschild. Alon and Trotter showed that, provided $c<1/4$, $f_c(n)\ll_c n^{1/2}$. Szemer\'{e}di observed that his regularity lemma implies that $f_c(n)\to \infty$. Edwards (unpublished) and Khadziivanov and Nikiforov \cite{KhNi79} proved independently that $f_c(n) \geq n/6$ when $c>1/4$ (see [905]). Fox and Loh \cite{FoLo12} proved that $ f_c(n) \leq n^…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #81

problems.title: Erdos Problem #82

problems.title: Erdos Problem #84

problems.title: Erdos Problem #87
problems.statement_preview: Let $\epsilon >0$. Is it true that, if $k$ is sufficiently large, then $ R(G)>(1-\epsilon)^kR(k) $ for every graph $G$ with chromatic number $\chi(G)=k$? problems.established_preview: No new proof; comparison-to-`R(k)` gap remains open.

problems.title: Erdos Problem #89

problems.references_preview: A $\sqrt{n}\times\sqrt{n}$ integer grid shows that this would be the best possible. problems.references_preview: The unit distance problem. In \cite{Er94b} Erdos dates this conjecture to 1946. In \cite{Er82e} he offers \$300 for the upper bound $n^{1+o(1)}$. This would be the best possible, as is shown by a set of lattice points. It is easy to show that there are $O(n^{3/2})$ many such pairs. problems.title: Erdos Problem #91
problems.statement_preview: Let $n$ be a sufficently large integer. Suppose $A\subset \mathbb{R}^2$ has $\lvert A\rvert=n$ and minimises the number of distinct distances between points in $A$. Prove that there are at least two (and probably many) such $A$ which are non-similar.
problems.references_preview: For $n=3$ the equilateral triangle is the only such set. For $n=4$ the square or two equilateral triangles sharing an edge give two non-similar examples. problems.references_preview: Conjectured by Erdos and Moser. In \cite{Er92e} Erdos credits the conjecture that the true upper bound is $2n$ to himself and Fishburn. problems.title: Erdos Problem #98

problems.title: Erdos Problem #100

problems.title: Erdos Problem #103
problems.statement_preview: Let $h(n)$ count the number of incongruent sets of $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ which minimise the diameter subject to the constraint that $d(x,y)\geq 1$ for all points $x eq y$. Is it true that $h(n)\to \infty$?

problems.title: Erdos Problem #130

problems.title: Erdos Problem #137

problems.title: Erdos Problem #141
problems.statement_preview: Let $k\geq 3$. Are there $k$ consecutive primes in arithmetic progression?
problems.references_preview: Green and Tao \cite{GrTa08} have proved that there must always exist some $k$ primes in arithmetic progression, but these need not be consecutive. It is open, even for $k=3$, whether there are infinitely many …

problems.title: Erdos Problem #142
problems.statement_preview: Let $r_k(N)$ be the largest possible size of a subset of $\{1,\ldots,N\}$ that does not contain any non-trivial $k$-term arithmetic progression. Prove an asymptotic formula for $r_k(N)$.

problems.title: Erdos Problem #143
problems.statement_preview: Let $A\subset (1,\infty)$ be a countably infinite set such that for all $x eq y\in A$ and integers $k\geq 1$ we have $ \lvert kx -y\rvert \geq 1. $ Does this imply that $A$ is sparse? problems.title: Erdos Problem #145

problems.title: Erdos Problem #149

problems.statement_preview: Let $n_1<\cdots < n_r\leq N$ with associated $a_i\pmod{n_i}$ such that the congruence classes are disjoint (that is, every integer is $\equiv a_i\pmod{n_i}$ for at most one $1\leq i\leq r$). How large can $r$ be in terms of $N$?
problems.references_preview: Let $f(N)$ be the maximum possible $r$. Erdos and Stein conjectured that $f(N)=o(N)$, which was proved by Erdos and Szemer\'{e}di \cite{ErSz68}, who showed that, for every $\epsilon>0$, $ \frac{N}{\exp((\log N)^{1/2+\epsilon})} \ll_\epsilon f(N) < \frac{N}{(\log N)^c} $ for some $c>0$. Erdos believed the lower bound is closer to the truth. These bounds were improved by Croot \…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #203
problems.statement_preview: Is there an integer $m\geq 1$ with $(m,6)=1$ such that none of $2^k3^\ell m+1$ are prime, for any $k,\ell\geq 0$?
problems.established_preview: Open; finite counterexample search found no candidate in the tested range.
problems.references_preview: Positive odd integers $m$ such that none of $2^km+1$ are prime are called Sierpinski numbers - see [1113] for more details. Erdos and Graham also ask more generally about $p_1^{k_1}\cdots p_r^{k_r}m+1$ for distinct primes $p_i$, or $q_1\cdots q_rm+1$ where the $q_i$ are primes congruent to $1\pmod{4}$. (Dogmachine has noted in the comments this latter question has the trivial …

problems.title: Erdos Problem #208
problems.statement_preview: Let $s_1<s_2<\cdots$ be the sequence of squarefree numbers. Is it true that, for any $\epsilon>0$ and large $n$, $ s_{n+1}-s_n \ll_\epsilon s_n^{\epsilon}? $ Is it true that $ s_{n+1}-s_n \leq (1+o(1))\frac{\pi^2}{6}\frac{\log s_n}{\log\log s_n}? $
problems.references_preview: Erdos \cite{Er51} showed that there are infinitely many $n$ such that $ s_{n+1}-s_n > (1+o(1))\frac{\pi^2}{6}\frac{\log s_n}{\log\log s_n}, $ so this bound would be the best possible. In \cite{Er79} Erdos says perhaps $s_{n+1}-s_n \ll \log s_n$, but he is 'very doubtful'. Filaseta and Trifonov \cite{FiTr92} proved an upper bound of $s_n^{1/5+o(1)}$. Pandey \cite{Pa24} has impr…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #212
problems.statement_preview: Is there a dense subset of $\mathbb{R}^2$ such that all pairwise distances are rational?
problems.references_preview: Conjectured by Ulam. Erdos believed there cannot be such a set. This problem is discussed in a blogpost by Terence Tao, in which he shows that there cannot be such a set, assuming the Bombieri-Lang conjecture. The same conclusion was independently obtained by Shaffaf \cite{Sh18}. Indeed, Shaffaf and Tao actually proved that such a rational distance set must be contained in a f…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #213
problems.statement_preview: Let $n\geq 4$. Are there $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$, no three on a line and no four on a circle, such that all pairwise distances are integers?
problems.references_preview: Anning and Erdos \cite{AnEr45} proved there cannot exist an infinite such set. Harborth constructed such a set when $n=5$. The best construction to date, due to Kreisel and Kurz \cite{KK08}, has $n=7$. Ascher, Braune, and Turchet \cite{ABT20} have shown that there is a uniform upper bound on the size of such a set, conditional on the Bombieri-Lang conjecture. Greenfeld, Iliopo…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #217
problems.statement_preview: For which $n$ are there $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$, no three on a line and no four on a circle, which determine $n-1$ distinct distances and so that (in some ordering of the distances) the $i$th distance occurs $i$ times?
problems.established_preview: No new witness or impossibility proof; open as stated.
problems.references_preview: An example with $n=4$ is an isosceles triangle with the point in the centre. Erdos originally believed this was impossible for $n\geq 5$, but Pomerance constructed a set with $n=5$ (see \cite{Er83c} for a description), and Pal\'{a}sti has proved such sets exist for all $n\leq 8$. Erdos believed this is impossible for all sufficiently large $n$. This would follow from $h(n)\geq…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #218
problems.statement_preview: Let $d_n=p_{n+1}-p_n$. The set of $n$ such that $d_{n+1}\geq d_n$ has density $1/2$, and similarly for $d_{n+1}\leq d_n$. Furthermore, there are infinitely many $n$ such that $d_{n+1}=d_n$.
problems.references_preview: In \cite{Er85c} Erdos also conjectures that $d_n=d_{n+1}=\cdots=d_{n+k}$ is solvable for every $k$ (which is equivalent to $k$ consecutive primes in arithmetic progression, see [141]). problems.statement_preview: Let $n_1<n_2<\cdots$ be the sequence of integers which are the sum of two squares. Explore the behaviour of (i.e. find good upper and lower bounds for) the consecutive differences $n_{k+1}-n_k$.
problems.references_preview: Erdos \cite{Er51} proved that, for infinitely many $k$, $ n_{k+1}-n_k \gg \frac{\log n_k}{\sqrt{\log\log n_k}}. $ Richards \cite{Ri82} improved this to $ \limsup_{k\to \infty} \frac{n_{k+1}-n_k}{\log n_k} \geq 1/4. $ The constant $1/4$ here has been improved, most lately to $0.868\cdots$ by Dietmann, Elsholtz, Kalmynin, Konyagin, and Maynard \cite{DEKKM22}. problems.title: Erdos Problem #233

problems.title: Erdos Problem #939

problems.statement_preview: Define $f:\mathbb{N}\to \mathbb{N}$ by $f(n)=n/2$ if $n$ is even and $f(n)=\frac{3n+1}{2}$ if $n$ is odd. Given any integer $m\geq 1$ does there exist $k\geq 1$ such that $f^{(k)}(m)=1$?
problems.references_preview: The infamous Collatz conjecture. For a detailed discussion of the history and theory surrounding this problem we refer to the overview by Lagarias \cite{La10}. This is not a problem due to Erdos; it was first devised by Collatz before 1952. Erdos referred to this problem on several occasions as 'hopeless'.
